1:
"Therefore thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest: for wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things." The witnesses note the inexcusable hypocrite who judges others. Gill notes some take this of the moral Gentiles (Socrates, Cato, Seneca) who reproved others yet did the same, and so are inexcusable; others take it of the Jews who despised and condemned the Gentiles. Barnes notes the appeal to their own conviction of right. The witnesses note Paul charging every man who judges another while doing the same — whether the moralizing Gentile or the proud Jew — as inexcusable, condemned by his own judgment; the hypocrite self-condemned. The witnesses note the inexcusable judge. The witnesses note self-condemnation.

The witnesses note the hypocrite who judges others while doing the same — inexcusable, self-condemned by his own judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the inexcusable hypocrite who judges others. The lesson the expositors draw is the self-condemnation of the hypocrite who judges in others what he does himself. The witnesses note the charge: "thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest" — Paul charges every man who judges another while doing the same; Gill notes some take this of the moralizing Gentiles (Socrates, Cato, Seneca) who reproved others yet did the same, others of the Jews who condemned the Gentiles; the inexcusable judge; the hypocrite addressed. The expositors observe the self-condemnation: "wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things" — in judging another, the hypocrite condemns himself, for he does the same; the self-condemnation; the judgment turned against the judge; the hypocrite caught in his own sentence. The witnesses press the inexcusableness: the man who knows enough to judge another's sin, yet does the same, is inexcusable — he knows the wrong, condemns it in another, yet commits it; the knowledge that aggravates; the inexcusable hypocrisy; "thou knewest... and yet." The expositors draw the lesson on the universality of the charge: the charge reaches all who judge — the moralist, the Jew, the religious; all who condemn in others what they do themselves; the self-righteous self-condemned; the universal hypocrisy exposed; the chapter's turn from the Gentiles (ch.1) to all who judge. The witnesses observe the appeal to conscience: Barnes notes the appeal to their own conviction of right — if they condemn the sin, their own conscience condemns their doing it; the conscience the witness; the self-condemnation by one's own standard. The expositors note the purpose in the argument: Paul, having exposed the Gentiles (ch.1), now turns to the self-righteous judge (especially the Jew), to bring all under sin (Romans 3:9, 19); the argument shutting every mouth; the universal guilt established; the way prepared for grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the inexcusable judge, the self-condemnation, and the hypocrisy exposed. The verse thus records the inexcusable hypocrite who judges others — condemning himself — teaching the self-condemnation of the hypocrite who judges in others what he does himself.
2:
"But we are sure that the judgment of God is according to truth against them which commit such things." The witnesses note the judgment of God according to truth. Gill notes "the judgment of God" here means not what is exercised in this life, but the judgment to come — certain, universal, carried on by the omniscient and omnipotent God, and definitive. Barnes notes "we are sure" (we know) — the common, admitted sentiment of mankind that God will punish such crimes. The witnesses note Paul affirming that God's judgment against evildoers is according to truth — not according to the partial, hypocritical judgment of men, but according to the real state of things; the certain, righteous judgment of God, searching the truth. The witnesses note judgment according to truth. The witnesses note the certain judgment.

The witnesses note God's judgment according to truth — not the partial judgment of men, but searching the real state of things.

So the witnesses make the verse the judgment of God according to truth. The lesson the expositors draw is the certain, righteous judgment of God, according to the real truth, not the partial judgment of men. The witnesses note the certainty: "we are sure that the judgment of God" — Barnes notes "we are sure" (we know) is the common, admitted sentiment of mankind that God will punish such crimes; Gill notes the judgment to come — certain, universal, definitive; the sure judgment; the known truth. The expositors observe the standard "according to truth": "is according to truth against them which commit such things" — God's judgment is according to truth — according to the real state of things, the actual guilt, not appearances; the true judgment; the searching of the reality; the judgment by the truth. The witnesses press the contrast with human judgment: the hypocrite judges partially, condemning in others what he excuses in himself; but God judges according to truth — impartially, searching the heart; the contrast of the partial human judgment and the true divine judgment; the truth against the partiality. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteousness of God's judgment: God's judgment is righteous, according to truth — no respecter of persons (v.11), no partiality, no excusing of the guilty; the just judgment; the truth searched; "shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?" (Genesis 18:25); the righteous judgment. The witnesses observe the inescapability for the hypocrite: the hypocrite, who escapes the partial judgment of men, cannot escape the true judgment of God; the judgment that searches the truth; the hypocrite exposed before God; the inescapable judgment. The expositors note the certainty grounding the warning: the certainty of God's true judgment grounds the warning to the self-righteous; the sure judgment a warning; the truth that will be searched; the judgment to be reckoned with. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the judgment according to truth, the certain judgment, and the searching of the real state. The verse thus records the judgment of God according to truth — against the evildoer — teaching the certain, righteous judgment of God, according to the real truth, not the partial judgment of men.
3:
"And thinkest thou this, O man, that judgest them which do such things, and doest the same, that thou shalt escape the judgment of God?" The witnesses note the vain presumption of escaping judgment. Barnes notes the appeal to common sense and the instinctive conviction of right: if they condemned those who practised these things — imperfect and unholy as their own sense of justice was — would not a holy and just God be far more likely to pronounce judgment? and could they escape who had delivered a similar sentence? The witnesses note Paul exposing the folly of the hypocrite who supposes he will escape the judgment he passes on others — the vain presumption of impunity; the self-deception that condemns others yet hopes to escape. The witnesses note the presumption of escape. The witnesses note the self-deception.

The witnesses note the vain presumption of escaping the judgment one passes on others — the self-deception of the hypocrite.

So the witnesses make the verse the vain presumption of escaping judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is the folly of the hypocrite who supposes he will escape the judgment he passes on others. The witnesses note the question: "thinkest thou this, O man, that judgest them which do such things, and doest the same, that thou shalt escape the judgment of God?" — Paul exposes the folly of the hypocrite who supposes he will escape; the vain presumption; the self-deception challenged. The expositors observe Barnes's appeal to common sense: if they condemned those who practised these things — imperfect and unholy as their own sense of justice was — would not a holy and just God be far more likely to pronounce judgment? the argument from the lesser to the greater; the human judge condemning, the divine Judge far more. The witnesses press the inconsistency: the hypocrite condemns others for the very sins he does, yet hopes to escape; the inconsistency; the self-deception that judges others but excuses self; the folly of the presumption; the contradiction exposed. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of judgment for the hypocrite: if the hypocrite's own (imperfect) sense of justice condemns the sin, far more will the holy God; the hypocrite's own judgment witnessing against him; the certainty of his condemnation; "out of thine own mouth will I judge thee" (Luke 19:22); the self-condemned. The witnesses observe the vanity of the presumption: the hope of escaping is vain — the hypocrite, condemned by his own judgment and by God's truth (v.2), cannot escape; the vain hope; the presumption of impunity exposed; the false security. The expositors note the warning against self-deception: the verse warns against the self-deception that condemns others yet presumes on impunity for oneself; the warning to the self-righteous; the danger of judging others while excusing self; the deception exposed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the presumption of escape, the self-deception, and the folly of the hypocrite. The verse thus records the vain presumption of escaping judgment — teaching the folly of the hypocrite who supposes he will escape the judgment he passes on others.
4:
"Or despisest thou the riches of his goodness and forbearance and longsuffering; not knowing that the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance?" The witnesses note God's goodness meant to lead to repentance. Gill notes Paul anticipates the objection from prosperity — that their prospering proved they were not so wicked, or would escape; but it was not their innocence, but the riches of God's goodness, longsuffering, and forbearance, that gave them their prosperity. Clarke and Barnes note the purpose of the goodness — to lead to repentance. The witnesses note Paul charging the presumptuous with despising God's riches of goodness, forbearance, and longsuffering, not knowing that this goodness was meant to lead them to repentance — the purpose of God's patience, and the sin of abusing it. The witnesses note the riches of goodness. The witnesses note goodness leading to repentance.

The witnesses note God's goodness, forbearance, and longsuffering meant to lead to repentance — and the sin of despising it.

So the witnesses make the verse God's goodness meant to lead to repentance. The lesson the expositors draw is the purpose of God's patience — to lead to repentance — and the sin of despising and abusing it. The witnesses note the riches: "the riches of his goodness and forbearance and longsuffering" — Paul speaks of the riches of God's goodness (his kindness in giving good things), forbearance (his holding back of judgment), and longsuffering (his patience with the sinner); the abundant patience; the riches of mercy; the divine forbearance. The expositors observe Gill's note on the objection answered: the prosperity of the wicked was not their innocence, but the riches of God's goodness and forbearance; the prosperity a token of patience, not approval; the goodness, not the merit; the patience misread as impunity. The witnesses press the purpose of the goodness: "not knowing that the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance" — the goodness of God is meant to lead to repentance; the design of the patience — to give space and motive for turning; the kindness that should melt the heart; the goodness drawing to repentance; "the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation" (2 Peter 3:9, 15). The expositors draw the lesson on the design of God's patience: God's forbearance is not approval, but a call to repentance; the patience that gives time to turn; the goodness meant to melt and draw; the design of mercy; the space for repentance. The witnesses observe the sin of despising it: "despisest thou the riches of his goodness" — to abuse God's patience as impunity, to harden under it rather than repent, is to despise the riches of his goodness; the sin of presuming on patience; the abuse of mercy; the goodness despised. The expositors note the aggravation of abusing mercy: to sin against God's goodness, to harden under his patience, aggravates the guilt; the mercy abused increasing the sin; the goodness turned to occasion of hardening; the aggravated guilt. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the riches of goodness, the purpose of leading to repentance, and the sin of despising it. The verse thus records God's goodness meant to lead to repentance — and the sin of despising it — teaching the purpose of God's patience and the guilt of abusing it.
5:
"But after thy hardness and impenitent heart treasurest up unto thyself wrath against the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God;" The witnesses note the treasuring up of wrath. Gill distinguishes natural hardness (in every son of Adam), acquired habitual hardness (increased by sinning), and judicial hardness (which God gives sinners up to for sin); an impenitent heart is one that cannot repent of itself. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the hard, impenitent heart, abusing God's patience, treasures up wrath against the day of wrath — the dreadful accumulation of judgment by every unrepented sin; the day of wrath when the righteous judgment of God will be revealed. The witnesses note the hard, impenitent heart. The witnesses note treasuring up wrath.

The witnesses note the hard heart treasuring up wrath against the day of wrath — the dreadful accumulation of judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the treasuring up of wrath. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadful accumulation of judgment by the hard, impenitent heart that abuses God's patience. The witnesses note the hard heart: "after thy hardness and impenitent heart" — Gill distinguishes natural hardness (in every son of Adam), acquired habitual hardness (increased by sinning), and judicial hardness (God giving the sinner up for sin); an impenitent heart that cannot of itself repent; the hard, unyielding heart; the impenitence. The expositors observe the treasuring up of wrath: "treasurest up unto thyself wrath against the day of wrath" — the hard heart, instead of repenting under God's goodness (v.4), treasures up wrath; the accumulation of judgment; every unrepented sin adding to the store; the dreadful hoard; the wrath laid up. The witnesses press the dreadful image: the sinner "treasures up" wrath — as a man stores treasure, so the impenitent stores up wrath against himself; the accumulating judgment; the store of wrath growing with every sin; the dreadful treasure; the wrath hoarded. The expositors draw the lesson on the abuse of patience increasing wrath: the very patience of God (v.4), abused, increases the wrath — for the hard heart, refusing to repent, adds to the store; the patience abused turned to greater judgment; the goodness despised increasing the wrath; the mercy refused aggravating the doom. The witnesses observe the day of wrath: "against the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God" — the wrath is stored against the day of wrath, when God's righteous judgment will be revealed; the day of reckoning; the revelation of the just judgment; the day when the hidden wrath breaks forth; "the great day of his wrath" (Revelation 6:17). The expositors note the righteousness of the judgment revealed: the day of wrath is the revelation of the righteous judgment — the just sentence, long delayed, then made manifest; the righteous judgment displayed; the justice vindicated; the truth revealed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hard heart, the treasuring up of wrath, and the day of righteous judgment. The verse thus records the treasuring up of wrath — by the impenitent heart — teaching the dreadful accumulation of judgment by the hard heart that abuses God's patience, against the day of wrath.
6:
"Who will render to every man according to his deeds:" The witnesses note the judgment according to works. Gill notes God will render to wicked men according to the demerit of their sins (eternal damnation), and to good men eternal life — not according to the merit of their good works (which have none), but according to their nature; those who believe in Christ and perform good works from a principle of grace shall receive the reward of the inheritance. The witnesses note Paul stating that God will render to every man according to his deeds — the principle of judgment according to works, the wicked condemned for their sins and the believer rewarded (not for merit, but as the fruit of grace); the impartial judgment by works. The witnesses note rendering according to deeds. The witnesses note works as evidence, not merit.

The witnesses note God rendering to every man according to his deeds — judgment by works, the wicked condemned, the believer rewarded by grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the judgment according to works. The lesson the expositors draw is the impartial judgment according to works, the wicked condemned for their sins and the believer rewarded as the fruit of grace, not merit. The witnesses note the principle: "who will render to every man according to his deeds" — God will judge every man according to his deeds; the universal principle; the judgment by works; the deeds the measure. The expositors observe Gill's careful distinction on merit: God will render to the wicked according to the demerit of their sins (eternal damnation), and to the good eternal life — not according to the merit of their good works (which have none), but according to their nature; the believer's works the fruit of grace, not the ground of reward; the works rewarded, but not as merit. The witnesses present the harmony with justification by faith: this judgment "according to works" does not contradict justification by faith; for the believer's works are the evidence and fruit of saving faith, not the meritorious ground; God judges according to works as the manifestation of the heart's state — the wicked's evil works showing their unbelief, the believer's good works showing his faith; the works the evidence of the man; "faith without works is dead" (James 2:26). The expositors draw the lesson on the consistency of the doctrine: justification is by faith alone, but the faith that justifies is not alone — it works; the judgment by works displays the reality of faith; the works the fruit, not the root; the reward of grace, not of debt; the harmony of grace and the judgment by works. The witnesses observe the impartiality: God renders to every man — without partiality; the wicked condemned, the righteous rewarded; the impartial judgment; the no-respect-of-persons (v.11); the even-handed reckoning. The expositors note the believer's reward as of grace: the believer's eternal life is rendered "according to" his works (their nature, as the fruit of grace), yet is itself the gift of grace; the reward of grace; the inheritance, not the wages; "the gift of God is eternal life" (Romans 6:23); the grace crowning its own work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rendering according to deeds, the works as evidence not merit, and the impartial judgment. The verse thus records God rendering to every man according to his deeds — teaching the impartial judgment according to works, the wicked condemned and the believer rewarded as the fruit of grace, not merit.
7:
"To them who by patient continuance in well doing seek for glory and honour and immortality, eternal life:" The witnesses note the reward of the persevering well-doer. Gill notes these words describe one sort of persons to whom God will render according to their works; not the best-moralized Gentiles (who sought worldly things and applause), nor the pharisaical Jews (who sought righteousness by works and honour from men), nor any unregenerate persons, but only those who have the true principles of grace implanted in them. The witnesses note Paul describing those who, by patient continuance in well doing, seek glory and immortality, to whom God renders eternal life — the persevering, grace-wrought obedience that issues in eternal life; the marks of the regenerate, not the natural man. The witnesses note patient continuance in well doing. The witnesses note eternal life rendered.

The witnesses note the reward of the persevering well-doer — eternal life to grace-wrought obedience, not the natural man's striving.

So the witnesses make the verse the reward of the persevering well-doer. The lesson the expositors draw is that eternal life is rendered to the grace-wrought, persevering obedience of the regenerate, not the striving of the natural man. The witnesses note the description: "them who by patient continuance in well doing seek for glory and honour and immortality" — Paul describes those who, by patient continuance in well doing, seek glory, honour, and immortality; the persevering well-doer; the steady obedience; the seeking of the eternal. The expositors observe Gill's careful limiting: these are not the best-moralized Gentiles (who sought worldly things and applause), nor the pharisaical Jews (who sought righteousness by works and honour from men), nor any unregenerate persons, but only those who have the true principles of grace implanted in them; the marks of the regenerate; the grace-wrought obedience; not the natural man. The witnesses press the perseverance: "patient continuance in well doing" — not a fitful or self-seeking obedience, but a patient, persevering well-doing; the steady continuance; the endurance in good; the marks of the true believer; "be thou faithful unto death" (Revelation 2:10). The expositors draw the lesson on the fruit of grace issuing in eternal life: the regenerate, by the grace implanted, persevere in well doing and seek the eternal; their obedience the fruit of grace, issuing in eternal life; the reward of grace to its own work; the eternal life rendered to the grace-wrought obedience; the harmony with grace. The witnesses observe the seeking of glory and immortality: the believer seeks glory, honour, and immortality — the eternal, not the worldly; the heart set on the things above; the seeking of the incorruptible; "seek those things which are above" (Colossians 3:1); the heavenly aim. The expositors note the reward: "eternal life" — God renders eternal life to such; the gift crowning the grace-wrought obedience; the inheritance; the eternal life; the reward of grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the patient continuance in well doing, the seeking of the eternal, and eternal life rendered to grace-wrought obedience. The verse thus records the reward of the persevering well-doer — eternal life — teaching that eternal life is rendered to the grace-wrought, persevering obedience of the regenerate.
8:
"But unto them that are contentious, and do not obey the truth, but obey unrighteousness, indignation and wrath," The witnesses note the doom of the contentious who disobey the truth. Gill notes "them that are of the contention" — who contend for victory and not truth, are quarrelsome, and sow discord. Barnes notes "contentious" here denotes a disposition toward God — rebellious, opposing God, contending with the Almighty and resisting his claims. The witnesses note Paul describing the other sort — the contentious who do not obey the truth but obey unrighteousness — to whom God renders indignation and wrath; the rebellious heart that resists God and serves sin, the opposite of the believing obedience; the doom of the disobedient. The witnesses note the contentious disobedient. The witnesses note indignation and wrath.

The witnesses note the doom of the contentious who disobey the truth and obey unrighteousness — indignation and wrath.

So the witnesses make the verse the doom of the contentious. The lesson the expositors draw is the wrath rendered to the rebellious heart that resists the truth and serves unrighteousness. The witnesses note the contentiousness: "them that are contentious" — Gill notes those who contend for victory and not truth, quarrelsome, sowing discord; Barnes notes the word here denotes a disposition toward God — rebellious, opposing God, contending with the Almighty and resisting his claims; the rebellious spirit; the contention against God. The expositors observe the disobedience to truth: "and do not obey the truth, but obey unrighteousness" — the contentious do not obey the truth (the gospel, the revealed will of God), but obey unrighteousness (serve sin); the disobedience to truth; the obedience to sin; the heart serving the wrong master. The witnesses press the contrast with the believer: the believer obeys the truth and continues in well doing (v.7); the contentious resist the truth and serve unrighteousness; the contrast of the two hearts; the obedience and the rebellion; the faith and the disobedience. The expositors draw the lesson on the rebellion of the heart: the contentious are not merely the quarrelsome among men, but the rebellious against God — resisting his truth, refusing his claims, serving sin; the rebellion of the heart; the disposition against God; the enmity to the truth. The witnesses observe the doom: "indignation and wrath" — God renders indignation and wrath to such; the divine displeasure; the wrath of God against the rebellious; the doom of the disobedient; the just recompense. The expositors note the moral root of the doom: the doom is rendered for the disposition — the resisting of truth, the serving of unrighteousness; the wrath against the rebellious heart; the doom rooted in the disobedience; the judgment by the heart's allegiance; the moral ground. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the contentious disobedient, the serving of unrighteousness, and the indignation and wrath. The verse thus records the doom of the contentious — indignation and wrath — teaching the wrath rendered to the rebellious heart that resists the truth and serves unrighteousness.
9:
"Tribulation and anguish, upon every soul of man that doeth evil, of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile;" The witnesses note the tribulation upon every evildoer, Jew and Gentile. Gill notes these words express the second death, the torments of hell, the worm that never dies. Barnes notes "tribulation" denotes affliction, being pressed down by punishment; "anguish" (a strong word) the extreme distress of the soul under the reward of sin. The witnesses note Paul declaring tribulation and anguish upon every evildoer — the Jew first, then the Gentile — the impartial doom of sin falling on all, with the Jew first in privilege and so first in accountability; the wrath upon every soul that does evil. The witnesses note tribulation and anguish. The witnesses note the Jew first.

The witnesses note tribulation and anguish upon every evildoer — the Jew first, then the Gentile; the impartial doom of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the tribulation upon every evildoer. The lesson the expositors draw is the impartial doom of sin falling on all, with the Jew first in privilege and so first in accountability. The witnesses note the doom: "tribulation and anguish, upon every soul of man that doeth evil" — Gill notes these express the second death, the torments of hell; Barnes notes "tribulation" the being pressed down by punishment, "anguish" the extreme distress of the soul; the doom of sin; the wrath on the evildoer. The expositors observe the universality: "upon every soul of man that doeth evil" — the doom falls on every soul that does evil, without exception; the universal doom; the wrath on all the wicked; no escape for any evildoer. The witnesses press the "Jew first": "of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile" — the Jew first, then the Gentile; the Jew, having the greater privilege (the law, the oracles), is first in accountability; the privilege increasing the responsibility; "to whom much is given, of him shall be much required" (Luke 12:48); the Jew first in judgment as in privilege. The expositors draw the lesson on privilege and accountability: the Jew's privilege (the law, the covenant) made him first in accountability; the greater light, the greater responsibility; the privilege bringing the greater reckoning; the Jew first in judgment. The witnesses observe the impartiality within the order: though the Jew is first, the doom falls also on the Gentile; the impartial judgment; the order of accountability, but the same doom on all evildoers; the no-respect-of-persons (v.11) within the order. The expositors note the warning to the privileged: the "Jew first" warns the privileged that their advantages, abused, bring the greater judgment; the warning to those with light; the privilege a peril if abused; the greater accountability. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the tribulation on the evildoer, the Jew first, and the impartial doom of sin. The verse thus records the tribulation upon every evildoer — Jew first, then Gentile — teaching the impartial doom of sin, with the Jew first in privilege and so first in accountability.
10:
"But glory, honour, and peace, to every man that worketh good, to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile:" The witnesses note the reward to every well-doer, Jew and Gentile. Gill notes glory, honour, and peace are words for the everlasting happiness of the saints — a crown of glory, an honour exceeding the greatest potentates, and a peace that passes all understanding. Clarke notes that while the impenitent experience indignation, every man that worketh good — that lives in obedience — receives glory and honour. The witnesses note Paul declaring glory, honour, and peace to every well-doer — the Jew first, then the Gentile — the reward of grace-wrought obedience falling impartially on all who do good, with the Jew first in privilege; the blessedness of the saints. The witnesses note glory, honour, and peace. The witnesses note the reward to every well-doer.

The witnesses note glory, honour, and peace to every well-doer — the Jew first, then the Gentile; the reward falling impartially.

So the witnesses make the verse the reward to every well-doer. The lesson the expositors draw is the reward of grace-wrought obedience falling impartially on all who do good, Jew and Gentile. The witnesses note the reward: "glory, honour, and peace, to every man that worketh good" — Gill notes glory, honour, and peace are words for the everlasting happiness of the saints — a crown of glory, an honour exceeding the greatest potentates, a peace passing understanding; the blessed reward; the threefold happiness. The expositors observe the universality: "to every man that worketh good" — the reward falls on every well-doer, without exception; the universal reward; the blessing on all the righteous; the impartial grace. The witnesses press the "Jew first": "to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile" — the Jew first, then the Gentile; the order of privilege; yet the reward extending also to the Gentile; the blessing impartial within the order; the Jew first, the Gentile also. The expositors draw the lesson on the impartial reward: as the doom falls on all evildoers (v.9), so the reward falls on all well-doers, Jew and Gentile; the impartial reward; the no-respect-of-persons (v.11); the grace extending to all; the reward without partiality. The witnesses observe the well-doing as grace-wrought: the "worketh good" is, as v.7, the grace-wrought obedience of the regenerate, not the natural man's striving; the good works the fruit of grace; the well-doing of the believer; the obedience of faith. The expositors note the threefold reward: glory (the manifested honour of the saints), honour (the exaltation above the potentates), and peace (the peace passing understanding); the full blessedness; the reward complete; the happiness of the saints. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the glory, honour, and peace, the Jew first, and the reward falling impartially. The verse thus records the reward to every well-doer — glory, honour, and peace — teaching the reward of grace-wrought obedience falling impartially on all who do good, Jew and Gentile.
11:
"For there is no respect of persons with God." The witnesses note the impartiality of God in judgment. Gill notes that at the day of judgment it will not come into consideration of what nation men are, from what parents descended, of what age, sex, state, or sect, nor whether they conformed to such externals; but only whether they are righteous men or sinners. The witnesses note Paul grounding the impartial judgment (v.9-10) in the truth that God is no respecter of persons — judging not by nation, privilege, or outward distinction, but by the real character; the impartiality that levels Jew and Gentile before the righteous Judge. The witnesses note no respect of persons. The witnesses note the impartial Judge.

The witnesses note that God is no respecter of persons — judging not by nation or privilege, but by the real character.

So the witnesses make the verse the impartiality of God in judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that God judges not by nation, privilege, or outward distinction, but by the real character. The witnesses note the principle: "there is no respect of persons with God" — God is no respecter of persons; the impartiality; the even-handed judgment; the ground of v.9-10. The expositors observe Gill's exposition of what does not count: at the judgment it will not come into consideration of what nation men are, from what parents descended, of what age, sex, state, or sect, nor whether they conformed to externals; but only whether they are righteous men or sinners; the outward distinctions set aside; the real character alone considered. The witnesses press the leveling of Jew and Gentile: the impartiality levels Jew and Gentile before the Judge — the Jew's privilege gives no exemption, the Gentile's lack no excuse; the same standard for all; the real character the measure; the privilege not the ground. The expositors draw the lesson on the impartial judgment: God judges by the truth (v.2), by the deeds (v.6), by the real character — not by nation, privilege, or appearance; the impartial Judge; the no-respect-of-persons; the judgment by reality; "God is no respecter of persons" (Acts 10:34). The witnesses observe the warning to the privileged: the impartiality warns the Jew (and the privileged) that their advantages will not exempt them; the privilege no shelter; the outward no defense; the warning to the self-confident; the impartiality a warning. The expositors note the comfort to the Gentile: the impartiality assures the Gentile that he is not excluded — the same reward for the well-doer, Jew or Gentile; the door open to all; the impartial grace; the comfort of the even judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no-respect-of-persons, the impartial Judge, and the judgment by real character. The verse thus records the impartiality of God — no respect of persons — teaching that God judges not by nation, privilege, or outward distinction, but by the real character.
12:
"For as many as have sinned without law shall also perish without law: and as many as have sinned in the law shall be judged by the law;" The witnesses note the judgment of each by the light he had. Gill notes the Gentiles, "without law" — the written law of Moses — yet not without the law of nature in their breasts, sinned against the God of nature and the light of nature, and so shall perish. Barnes notes this shows the principle on which God treats men so as not to be a respecter of persons. The witnesses note Paul declaring that those who sinned without the written law perish without it (judged by the light of nature), and those under the law are judged by it — the impartial principle that each is judged by the light he had; the Gentile by nature, the Jew by the law, both justly condemned. The witnesses note judged by the light had. The witnesses note Gentile and Jew.

The witnesses note each judged by the light he had — the Gentile by nature, the Jew by the law; both justly condemned.

So the witnesses make the verse the judgment of each by his light. The lesson the expositors draw is the impartial principle that each is judged by the light he had, the Gentile by nature and the Jew by the law. The witnesses note the Gentile without law: "as many as have sinned without law shall also perish without law" — Gill notes the Gentiles, "without law" (the written law of Moses), yet not without the law of nature in their breasts, sinned against the God of nature and the light of nature, and so shall perish; the Gentile judged by the light of nature; the perishing without the written law. The expositors observe the Jew under the law: "as many as have sinned in the law shall be judged by the law" — those under the law (the Jews) are judged by the law they had; the Jew judged by the written law; the standard he possessed; the judgment by the law. The witnesses press the impartial principle: each is judged by the light he had — the Gentile by nature, the Jew by the law; the impartial principle; the judgment proportioned to the light; the no-respect-of-persons (v.11) applied; the fair standard for each. The expositors draw the lesson on the justice of judging by light: God judges each by the light he possessed — the Gentile not condemned for the written law he never had, but for the light of nature he sinned against; the Jew judged by the law he had; the just judgment; the light the measure; the fairness of God. The witnesses observe the condemnation of both: both Gentile and Jew are justly condemned — the Gentile for sinning against nature, the Jew for sinning against the law; both under sin; both guilty; the universal guilt; "all the world... guilty before God" (Romans 3:19). The expositors note the establishing of universal guilt: the verse establishes that all — Gentile and Jew — are justly condemned, each by his light; the universal guilt; the way prepared for the gospel; all shut up under sin; the argument toward grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the judgment by the light had, the Gentile and the Jew, and the just condemnation of both. The verse thus records the judgment of each by his light — the Gentile without law, the Jew by the law — teaching the impartial principle that each is judged by the light he had, both justly condemned.
13:
"(For not the hearers of the law are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified." The witnesses note that doers, not hearers, are justified. Gill notes the apostle shows the Jews were justly condemned despite having and hearing the law, since hearing without doing will never make a person righteous in God's sight; the bare hearing of the law every sabbath, without practical obedience, falls greatly short. Barnes notes the general principle that the doers of the law only can be justified, if justification is attempted by the law. The witnesses note Paul stating that not the hearers but the doers of the law are justified — the principle that the law demands doing, not mere hearing; a principle that condemns the Jew (a hearer but not a doer) and that, since none perfectly do the law, drives all to Christ. The witnesses note doers, not hearers. The witnesses note the law's demand.

The witnesses note that doers, not hearers, are justified — the law demands doing, condemning the hearer, driving all to Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the principle that doers, not hearers, are justified. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law demands doing, not mere hearing — a principle that condemns the Jewish hearer and, since none perfectly do, drives all to Christ. The witnesses note the principle: "not the hearers of the law are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified" — Gill notes hearing without doing will never make a person righteous before God; Barnes notes the general principle that the doers of the law only can be justified, if justification is by the law; the law demands doing; the doers justified. The expositors observe the condemnation of the Jewish hearer: the Jews had and heard the law, boasting in it; but hearing without doing justifies none; the bare hearing every sabbath, without obedience, falls short; the Jew, a hearer but not a doer, condemned; the privilege of hearing no justification. The witnesses press the demand of the law: the law demands doing, perfect obedience — not mere hearing or knowing; the standard of the law; the doing required; "cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written" (Galatians 3:10); the law's exacting demand. The expositors draw the lesson on the law driving to Christ: since the law demands perfect doing, and none perfectly do it (Romans 3:23), the principle "the doers shall be justified" condemns all who seek justification by the law; the law that no one keeps; the principle that drives to Christ for the righteousness the law cannot give; "by the deeds of the law shall no flesh be justified" (Romans 3:20); the law the schoolmaster to Christ. The witnesses observe the hypothetical nature of the justification by doing: "the doers of the law shall be justified" states the law's principle — if any perfectly did it, he would be justified; but none does, so none is justified by the law; the hypothetical; the standard that condemns; the door closed by works, opened by faith. The expositors note the consistency with justification by faith: this does not teach justification by works, but states the law's standard, to show that none meets it, and so to drive to the gospel; the law's principle establishing the need of grace; the consistency with Romans 3-4; the doctrine harmonized. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the doers not hearers, the law's demand, and the driving to Christ. The verse thus records the principle that doers, not hearers, are justified — teaching that the law demands doing, condemning the hearer and, since none perfectly do, driving all to Christ.
14:
"For when the Gentiles, which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law, these, having not the law, are a law unto themselves:" The witnesses note the Gentiles' natural knowledge of the law. Gill notes the apostle owns the Gentiles had not the written law of Moses, yet intimates they had a law against which they sinned, and which they in part obeyed. Barnes notes the same principle (v.13) applies to the heathen. The witnesses note Paul observing that the Gentiles, without the written law, doing by nature the things of the law, are a law to themselves — the witness of natural conscience and moral knowledge in the heathen, enough to make them accountable; not a way of salvation, but a proof of their responsibility before God. The witnesses note the Gentiles a law to themselves. The witnesses note natural conscience.

The witnesses note the Gentiles doing by nature the things of the law — a law to themselves; the witness of natural conscience.

So the witnesses make the verse the Gentiles' natural knowledge of the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the witness of natural conscience and moral knowledge in the heathen, enough to make them accountable. The witnesses note the observation: "the Gentiles, which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law" — Gill notes the apostle owns the Gentiles had not the written law of Moses, yet they had a law against which they sinned and which they in part obeyed; the Gentiles doing by nature some things of the law; the natural moral knowledge. The expositors observe the "law unto themselves": "these, having not the law, are a law unto themselves" — the Gentiles, without the written law, are a law to themselves — their own conscience and moral sense supplying a rule; the inward law; the natural conscience the standard; the law within. The witnesses weigh whom this describes: most take it of the pagan Gentiles, who by natural conscience do some of the things of the law (honouring parents, restraining theft), showing they have a moral law within; some (as Augustine and others) have taken it of Gentile Christians who do the law by grace; the witnesses note both, but the chief sense is the natural conscience of the heathen, establishing their accountability. The expositors draw the lesson on natural conscience: the Gentiles have a natural conscience and moral knowledge — enough to know right from wrong, to be a law to themselves; the witness of conscience; the moral sense in all men; the law written on the heart (v.15); the universal moral knowledge. The witnesses observe that this is not a way of salvation: the natural conscience does not save — for the Gentiles sin against it (Romans 1:21, 32); rather, it proves their accountability, makes them inexcusable; not a door to heaven, but a ground of judgment; the conscience condemning, not saving. The expositors note the establishing of Gentile accountability: the natural law in the Gentiles establishes that they too are accountable, justly judged (v.12); the responsibility of all men; the heathen not excused for want of the written law; the universal accountability. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Gentiles a law to themselves, the natural conscience, and the accountability it establishes. The verse thus records the Gentiles doing by nature the things of the law — a law to themselves — teaching the witness of natural conscience, enough to make the heathen accountable.
15:
"Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and their thoughts the mean while accusing or else excusing one another;)" The witnesses note the law written on the heart and the witness of conscience. Gill notes that though the Gentiles had not the law in form, written on tables, they had "the work", the matter and substance of it in their minds, as appears by the practices of many of them; the moral law was written on Adam's heart at creation, obliterated by the fall, and the Gentiles retain its remnants. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the Gentiles show the work of the law written on their hearts, their conscience bearing witness, and their thoughts accusing or excusing — the inward witness of the moral law and the active conscience; the proof of their moral knowledge and accountability. The witnesses note the law written on the heart. The witnesses note conscience bearing witness.

The witnesses note the work of the law written on the heart and conscience bearing witness — the inward proof of moral accountability.

So the witnesses make the verse the law written on the heart and the witness of conscience. The lesson the expositors draw is the inward witness of the moral law and the active conscience, the proof of the Gentiles' moral knowledge and accountability. The witnesses note the law on the heart: "shew the work of the law written in their hearts" — Gill notes that though the Gentiles had not the law written on tables, they had "the work" — the matter and substance of it in their minds, as their practices show; the moral law written on Adam's heart at creation, obliterated by the fall, its remnants retained; the inward law; the moral substance within. The expositors observe the witness of conscience: "their conscience also bearing witness" — the conscience bears witness, testifying to the moral law within; the conscience the inward monitor; the witness to right and wrong; the voice within; the moral sense active. The witnesses press the accusing and excusing thoughts: "their thoughts the mean while accusing or else excusing one another" — the thoughts accuse (when conscience condemns the wrong) or excuse (when it approves the right); the inward debate; the conscience judging the man's own acts; the moral self-judgment; the active conscience. The expositors draw the lesson on the inward witness: the Gentiles have the work of the law within — the moral knowledge, the conscience, the accusing and excusing thoughts; the inward witness of accountability; the moral law not absent even from the heathen; the universal conscience; the proof of moral knowledge. The expositors note the establishing of accountability: the inward law and conscience establish that the Gentiles are accountable — they know enough to be judged, inexcusable (Romans 1:20); the accountability proved; the conscience the witness against them; the inward law condemning. The witnesses observe the contrast with the law written in regeneration: the law written on the heart here (the natural conscience) differs from the law written on the heart in regeneration (Jeremiah 31:33; the new covenant); the natural remnant and the gracious renewal; the conscience and the new heart; the two writings distinguished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the law on the heart, the conscience bearing witness, and the inward proof of accountability. The verse thus records the work of the law written on the heart and the witness of conscience — teaching the inward witness of the moral law, the proof of the Gentiles' moral knowledge and accountability.
16:
"In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel." The witnesses note the day of judgment by Jesus Christ. Gill notes this is to be read in connection with v.12, expressing the time when both Jews and Gentiles will be judged — a "day" both for the clearness of the judgment and for the certain (though unknown) time fixed. Barnes notes the intermediate verses are a parenthesis; God will judge all in righteousness, the Jew by the law and the heathen by conscience. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God will judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ, according to the gospel — the searching judgment of the hidden things, committed to Christ the Judge, a truth belonging to Paul's gospel; the day that gathers the whole argument. The witnesses note the judgment of secrets. The witnesses note Christ the Judge.

The witnesses note the day God will judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ — the searching judgment of the hidden things.

So the witnesses make the verse the day of judgment by Jesus Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the searching judgment of the hidden things, committed to Christ, according to the gospel. The witnesses note the day: "in the day when God shall judge" — Gill notes this connects with v.12, expressing the time when both Jews and Gentiles will be judged; Barnes notes the intermediate verses (13-15) are a parenthesis; the day of judgment; the appointed time; the day fixed. The expositors observe the searching of secrets: "God shall judge the secrets of men" — God will judge the secrets — the hidden thoughts, motives, and deeds; the searching judgment; nothing hidden from the Judge; "the LORD searcheth all hearts" (1 Chronicles 28:9); the secrets revealed. The witnesses press the judgment by Jesus Christ: "by Jesus Christ" — the judgment is committed to Jesus Christ; Christ the Judge of all (John 5:22; Acts 17:31); the Son appointed to judge; the judgment in his hands; the Judge revealed. The expositors draw the lesson on the searching judgment: God will judge not only the outward acts, but the secrets — the hidden thoughts and motives; the searching, perfect judgment; the heart laid bare; the secrets that men hide brought to light; the inescapable scrutiny. The witnesses observe "according to my gospel": "according to my gospel" — the truth of the judgment by Christ belongs to Paul's gospel; the gospel that proclaims Christ the Judge as well as the Saviour; the judgment a part of the gospel message; the gospel including the reckoning; Paul's gospel. The expositors note the gathering of the argument: the verse gathers the argument (v.1-15) — all, Jew and Gentile, will be judged by God through Christ, by the truth, according to their light and works, the secrets searched; the day that concludes the argument; the universal judgment; the reckoning that establishes the need of the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the judgment of secrets, Christ the Judge, and the day according to the gospel. The verse thus records the day God will judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ — teaching the searching judgment of the hidden things, committed to Christ, according to the gospel.
17:
"Behold, thou art called a Jew, and restest in the law, and makest thy boast of God," The witnesses note the Jew's confidence in his privileges. Chrysostom notes Paul sets down the glories of the Jews, owing to which they thought scorn of the Gentiles — first the very name, which was of great majesty (as the name Christian is now); yet Paul does not say "thou art a Jew" but "art called" so, "and makest thy boast in God," as being loved and honoured above all men; "here he seems to me to be gently mocking" the boast. The witnesses note Paul rehearsing the Jew's confidence — his name, his resting in the law, his boasting in God — the privileges in which the Jew trusted, soon to be turned against him; the boast that would be exposed by his conduct. The witnesses note the Jew's privileges. The witnesses note the boast in God.

The witnesses note the Jew resting in the law and boasting in God — the privileges in which he trusted, soon turned against him.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jew's confidence in his privileges. The lesson the expositors draw is the rehearsal of the Jew's boasted privileges, soon to be exposed by his conduct. The witnesses note the name: "thou art called a Jew" — Chrysostom notes the name was of great majesty (as the name Christian is now); the honoured name; yet Paul says "art called" a Jew, not "art" a Jew; the name boasted in; the title of privilege. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the gentle mockery: Paul does not say "thou art a Jew" but "art called" so, "and makest thy boast in God," as being loved and honoured above all men; Chrysostom sees Paul gently mocking the boast — the name without the reality; the title without the substance; the gentle irony. The witnesses press the resting in the law: "restest in the law" — the Jew rested in the law — relying on the possession of it, the having and hearing of it, as his security; the false rest; the trusting in the privilege; the resting on the having, not the doing (v.13). The expositors draw the lesson on the boast in God: "makest thy boast of God" — the Jew boasted in God, as his God, loving and honouring him above all men; the boast of relationship; the confidence in the privilege; yet the boast empty without obedience; the boast to be tested. The witnesses observe the privileges trusted: the name, the law, the relationship to God — the privileges in which the Jew trusted; the grounds of his confidence; the advantages he leaned on; the privileges boasted. The expositors note the turning of the boast: Paul rehearses the boast (v.17-20) only to turn it against the Jew (v.21-24) — for the privileges, abused, condemn rather than save; the boast exposed; the privilege without obedience a reproach; the confidence overturned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jew's privileges, the boast in God, and the confidence soon to be tested. The verse thus records the Jew resting in the law and boasting in God — teaching the rehearsal of the boasted privileges, soon to be exposed by his conduct.
18:
"And knowest his will, and approvest the things that are more excellent, being instructed out of the law;" The witnesses note the Jew's knowledge of God's will. Gill notes "his will" is not the secret will, nor the gospel (of which they were ignorant), but his declared will in the law, showing what is and is not to be done; to know this was a privilege other peoples had not, but the bare knowledge is of no avail, for persons may know their Lord's will and not do it. Barnes notes this knowledge came from the Scriptures. The witnesses note Paul granting that the Jew knows God's will and approves the excellent things, being instructed by the law — the real privilege of knowledge, yet a knowledge that condemns if not obeyed; the light that aggravates the disobedience. The witnesses note knowing God's will. The witnesses note knowledge without doing.

The witnesses note the Jew knowing God's will and approving the excellent — a real privilege, yet condemning if not obeyed.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jew's knowledge of God's will. The lesson the expositors draw is the real privilege of knowledge, yet a knowledge that condemns if not obeyed. The witnesses note the knowledge: "knowest his will" — Gill notes "his will" is the declared will in the law (showing what is and is not to be done), not the secret will nor the gospel; Barnes notes this knowledge came from the Scriptures; the Jew's knowledge of God's revealed will; the real privilege; the light possessed. The expositors observe the approving of the excellent: "approvest the things that are more excellent, being instructed out of the law" — the Jew approves the excellent things, being taught by the law; the discernment of the better; the moral approval; the instructed judgment; the trained discernment. The witnesses press the privilege of knowledge: to know God's will and approve the excellent was a real privilege other peoples lacked; the advantage of the Scriptures; the light of revelation; the privilege of knowledge; the favour of the oracles (Romans 3:2). The expositors draw the lesson on knowledge without doing: Gill notes the bare knowledge is of no avail, for persons may know their Lord's will and not do it (and so be beaten with many stripes, Luke 12:47); the knowledge that condemns if not obeyed; the light that aggravates the disobedience; "to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin" (James 4:17); the privilege turned to a greater account. The witnesses observe the aggravation of knowledge: the Jew's knowledge, far from excusing, aggravates his disobedience — for he sins against the light; the greater light, the greater guilt; the knowledge increasing the accountability; the privilege a peril if abused. The expositors note the contrast with doing: the Jew knows and approves, but (v.21-23) does not do; the knowledge without the practice; the approval without the obedience; the light without the walk; the privilege without the fruit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the knowing God's will, the approving the excellent, and the knowledge that condemns if not obeyed. The verse thus records the Jew knowing God's will and approving the excellent — teaching the real privilege of knowledge, yet a knowledge that condemns if not obeyed.
19:
"And art confident that thou thyself art a guide of the blind, a light of them which are in darkness," The witnesses note the Jew's confidence as a teacher of others. Barnes notes "art confident" denotes the full assurance of the Jew that he was superior in knowledge to all others; it is a remarkable fact that the Jews put the fullest confidence in their religion — though proud and hypocritical, they were not speculative infidels, but had the fullest assurance that God was the Author of their institutions. The witnesses note Paul rehearsing the Jew's confidence that he is a guide of the blind and a light to those in darkness — the lofty self-estimation as the teacher of the nations; the pride that would soon be exposed by his failure to teach himself. The witnesses note the confident guide. The witnesses note the self-estimation.

The witnesses note the Jew confident as a guide of the blind and a light in darkness — the lofty self-estimation soon exposed.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jew's confidence as a teacher. The lesson the expositors draw is the lofty self-estimation of the Jew as the teacher of the nations, soon to be exposed by his failure to teach himself. The witnesses note the confidence: "art confident that thou thyself art a guide of the blind" — Barnes notes "art confident" denotes the full assurance of the Jew that he was superior in knowledge to all others; the confident self-estimation; the assurance of superiority; the pride of knowledge. The expositors observe the role claimed: "a guide of the blind, a light of them which are in darkness" — the Jew claimed to be a guide of the blind (the Gentiles, ignorant of God) and a light to those in darkness; the role of teacher of the nations; the self-appointed instructor; the lofty office claimed. The witnesses press the lofty self-estimation: the Jew esteemed himself the teacher of the nations — the guide, the light, the instructor; the high self-regard; the pride of the privileged; the self-estimation as the teacher of all; the confidence overweening. The expositors draw the lesson on the confidence of the Jew: Barnes notes the remarkable fact that the Jews put the fullest confidence in their religion — though proud and hypocritical, not speculative infidels, but fully assured that God was the Author of their institutions; the firm confidence; the assurance of their privilege; the confidence misplaced when divorced from obedience. The witnesses observe the truth in the claim: there was truth in the Jew's privilege — he did have the oracles, the light, the knowledge; the real advantage; yet the privilege boasted without the obedience; the truth abused; the light not walked in. The expositors note the pride to be exposed: the lofty self-estimation (v.19-20) would be exposed by the Jew's failure to teach himself (v.21); the pride humbled; the teacher who would not learn; the self-estimation overturned; the boast deflated. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the confident guide, the self-estimation, and the pride soon exposed. The verse thus records the Jew confident as a guide of the blind — teaching the lofty self-estimation of the Jew as the teacher of the nations, soon to be exposed by his failure to teach himself.
20:
"An instructor of the foolish, a teacher of babes, which hast the form of knowledge and of the truth in the law." The witnesses note the Jew's claim as instructor and his form of knowledge. Gill notes "an instructor of the foolish" — the common people among the Jews who knew not the law; it was usual to call their doctors and masters "an instructor." Barnes notes "the foolish" here means those void of understanding, whom the Jew esteemed himself qualified to teach; "babes" is figurative. The witnesses note Paul rehearsing the Jew's claim to instruct the ignorant and teach the simple, having the form of knowledge and truth in the law — the outward form and framework of knowledge, yet only the form; the shape of truth without the power and the practice. The witnesses note the instructor of the foolish. The witnesses note the form of knowledge.

The witnesses note the Jew's claim as instructor, having the form of knowledge — the shape of truth without the power and practice.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jew's claim as instructor and his form of knowledge. The lesson the expositors draw is the outward form of knowledge, the shape of truth without the power and the practice. The witnesses note the claim to instruct: "an instructor of the foolish, a teacher of babes" — Gill notes "instructor of the foolish" refers to the common people who knew not the law (the Jewish doctors called "instructors"); Barnes notes "the foolish" means those void of understanding, "babes" the figurative simple; the Jew's claim to teach the ignorant; the role of instructor. The expositors observe the form of knowledge: "which hast the form of knowledge and of the truth in the law" — the Jew had "the form" of knowledge and truth in the law; the outward form; the framework, the shape; the systematic knowledge; the form possessed. The witnesses press the significance of "the form": the Jew had the form of knowledge — the outward shape, the framework, the system — yet only the form; the shape without the substance; the form without the power; the knowledge without the practice; the appearance of truth. The expositors draw the lesson on the form without the power: the Jew had the form of knowledge and truth, but not the power — the outward framework without the inward reality and the obedient walk; the form without the godliness (2 Timothy 3:5); the shape of truth without its transforming power; the letter without the spirit (v.29). The witnesses observe the truth possessed in form: the Jew did possess the truth in the law — in its outward form; the real truth, but held in form only; the truth without the practice; the knowledge unapplied; the form of the truth. The expositors note the indictment implied: the rehearsal of the Jew's form of knowledge (v.17-20) sets up the indictment (v.21-24) — the form without the practice, the teacher who does not do; the form exposed; the knowledge that condemns; the indictment prepared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the instructor of the foolish, the form of knowledge, and the shape of truth without the power. The verse thus records the Jew's claim as instructor and his form of knowledge — teaching the outward form of knowledge, the shape of truth without the power and the practice.
21:
"Thou therefore which teachest another, teachest thou not thyself? thou that preachest a man should not steal, dost thou steal?" The witnesses note the turning of the indictment — the teacher who does not teach himself. Gill notes that though these are put as questions, they are to be taken as assertions and matters of fact; the Jewish teachers, though they taught others, did not teach themselves — blind leaders of the blind, ignorant of the spirituality of the law. The witnesses note Paul turning the boast against the Jew — he who teaches another does not teach himself; he who preaches against stealing steals — the hypocrisy of the teacher who does not practice what he preaches; the indictment exposing the gap between the profession and the practice. The witnesses note the teacher who fails himself. The witnesses note the hypocrisy exposed.

The witnesses note the teacher who does not teach himself — preaching against stealing yet stealing; the hypocrisy exposed.

So the witnesses make the verse the turning of the indictment. The lesson the expositors draw is the hypocrisy of the teacher who does not practice what he preaches. The witnesses note the turning: "thou therefore which teachest another, teachest thou not thyself?" — Paul turns the boast against the Jew; Gill notes these questions are to be taken as assertions of fact — the Jewish teachers taught others but not themselves, blind leaders of the blind; the teacher who fails himself; the indictment begun. The expositors observe the first charge: "thou that preachest a man should not steal, dost thou steal?" — the Jew preaches against stealing, yet steals; the contradiction; the preaching against the sin he commits; the hypocrisy; the practice belying the profession. The witnesses press the gap between profession and practice: the Jew teaches and preaches the law, yet does not keep it himself; the gap between the profession and the practice; the teacher who does not do; the hypocrisy of the unpracticed teaching; the words against the works. The expositors draw the lesson on the hypocrisy of the teacher: the teacher who does not practice what he preaches is a hypocrite — condemning in his words what he does in his deeds; the hypocrisy; the self-condemnation (v.1); the teacher self-judged; "physician, heal thyself" (Luke 4:23); the unpracticed teaching. The witnesses observe the assertion behind the question: Gill notes the questions are assertions of fact — the Jews did these things; the indictment not hypothetical, but real; the charge grounded in fact; the actual hypocrisy; the real contradiction. The expositors note the dishonour to the teaching: the teacher's sin dishonours his teaching — the law he professes is reproached by his breaking it; the teaching discredited; the profession shamed by the practice; the dishonour to the truth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the teacher who fails himself, the preaching against stealing yet stealing, and the hypocrisy exposed. The verse thus records the teacher who does not teach himself — preaching against stealing yet stealing — teaching the hypocrisy of the teacher who does not practice what he preaches.
22:
"Thou that sayest a man should not commit adultery, dost thou commit adultery? thou that abhorrest idols, dost thou commit sacrilege?" The witnesses note the further charges of adultery and sacrilege. Gill notes the adultery here is to be taken literally, not figuratively. Barnes notes there is no doubt this was a crime common among the Jews — the Talmud accuses some of their celebrated rabbis of it; and that abhorring idols, they yet committed sacrilege. The witnesses note Paul charging the Jew who forbids adultery with committing it, and the Jew who abhors idols with sacrilege — the further exposure of the gap between profession and practice; the hypocrisy in the very sins the Jew condemned; the boast belied by the deeds. The witnesses note adultery condemned yet committed. The witnesses note sacrilege.

The witnesses note the Jew forbidding adultery yet committing it, abhorring idols yet committing sacrilege — the hypocrisy exposed.

So the witnesses make the verse the further charges of adultery and sacrilege. The lesson the expositors draw is the further exposure of the gap between the Jew's profession and his practice. The witnesses note the charge of adultery: "thou that sayest a man should not commit adultery, dost thou commit adultery?" — the Jew forbids adultery yet commits it; Gill notes the adultery is literal; Barnes notes there is no doubt this was common among the Jews (the Talmud accusing some celebrated rabbis); the contradiction; the sin condemned yet committed. The expositors observe the charge of sacrilege: "thou that abhorrest idols, dost thou commit sacrilege?" — the Jew abhors idols yet commits sacrilege (robbing temples, or profaning sacred things, or robbing God in tithes, Malachi 3:8); Barnes notes the abhorring of idols joined to sacrilege; the contradiction; the profession against the practice. The witnesses press the hypocrisy in the condemned sins: the Jew committed the very sins he condemned — adultery, sacrilege; the hypocrisy in the very things professed against; the boast belied by the deeds; the contradiction complete; the words against the works. The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of the hypocrisy: the Jew's hypocrisy reached even the gravest sins — adultery, sacrilege; the deep contradiction; the profession of holiness with the practice of sin; the hypocrisy in the great matters; the boast hollow. The witnesses observe the abhorrence of idols joined to sacrilege: the Jew abhorred idols (the outward idolatry), yet committed sacrilege (robbing God or his temple); the inconsistency; the abhorring of one sin while committing a kindred one; the partial conscience; the selective religion. The expositors note the exposure of the boast: these charges (v.21-22) expose the Jew's boast (v.17-20) — the teacher of others a breaker of the law; the boast belied; the privilege turned to reproach; the self-righteousness exposed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the adultery condemned yet committed, the sacrilege, and the hypocrisy in the condemned sins. The verse thus records the Jew forbidding adultery yet committing it, abhorring idols yet committing sacrilege — teaching the further exposure of the gap between profession and practice.
23:
"Thou that makest thy boast of the law, through breaking the law dishonourest thou God?" The witnesses note the dishonouring of God by breaking the law. Gill notes the Jew boasted of having the law, of his knowledge of it, and even of keeping it to perfection. Barnes notes that to boast in the law implied conviction of its excellence and obligation; by boasting they proclaimed it was from God, and by breaking it they denied it — and since actions are the true test of men's opinions, their breaking the law dishonoured God more than their boasting honoured him. The witnesses note Paul charging that the Jew, who boasts in the law, dishonours God by breaking it — the contradiction between the boast and the breach; the law professed but transgressed, bringing dishonour on its Author. The witnesses note boasting and breaking. The witnesses note dishonouring God.

The witnesses note the Jew boasting in the law yet dishonouring God by breaking it — the contradiction of the boast and the breach.

So the witnesses make the verse the dishonouring of God by breaking the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the contradiction between the boast in the law and the breach of it, bringing dishonour on its Author. The witnesses note the boast: "thou that makest thy boast of the law" — Gill notes the Jew boasted of having the law, of his knowledge of it, and even of keeping it to perfection; the boast in the law; the glorying in the possession; the confidence in the privilege. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the boast and the denial: to boast in the law implied conviction of its excellence and obligation (one does not boast of what he esteems worthless); by boasting they proclaimed it was from God, and by breaking it they denied it; the boast and the breach contradicting; the profession and the practice opposed. The witnesses press the dishonour to God: "through breaking the law dishonourest thou God?" — by breaking the law, the Jew dishonours God; the law transgressed dishonouring its Author; the breach a dishonour; the sin against the law a reproach to God. The expositors draw the lesson on actions as the test: Barnes notes that actions are the true test of men's real opinions — the Jew's breaking the law dishonoured God more than his boasting honoured him; the deeds the true witness; the practice outweighing the profession; the actions speaking louder than the boast; the conduct the test. The witnesses observe the contradiction: the Jew boasts in the law (honouring it in word) yet breaks it (dishonouring God in deed); the contradiction; the boast and the breach; the word and the work at odds; the hypocrisy complete. The expositors note the dishonour greater than the honour: the breaking of the law dishonoured God more than the boasting honoured him — for the deeds outweigh the words; the dishonour exceeding the honour; the practice the louder witness; the reproach greater than the praise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the boasting and breaking, the dishonouring of God, and the actions as the test. The verse thus records the Jew boasting in the law yet dishonouring God by breaking it — teaching the contradiction of the boast and the breach, bringing dishonour on the Author of the law.
24:
"For the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles through you, as it is written." The witnesses note the blasphemy of God's name through the Jews' sin. Gill notes the being and perfections of God, and his law and worship, are denied or evil spoken of. Barnes notes the Jews' conduct led the heathen world to blaspheme and reproach both their religion and its Author — by their hypocrisy and crimes the pagan world was led to despise a religion observed to have no effect in purifying its professors, and the reproach terminated on its Author. The witnesses note Paul charging that, through the Jews' hypocrisy, the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles — the dreadful effect of the professing people's sin, bringing reproach on God himself; the inconsistent life dishonouring the holy name. The witnesses note God's name blasphemed. The witnesses note reproach through hypocrisy.

The witnesses note God's name blasphemed among the Gentiles through the Jews' sin — the inconsistent life dishonouring the holy name.

So the witnesses make the verse the blasphemy of God's name through the Jews' sin. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadful effect of the professing people's sin, bringing reproach on God himself. The witnesses note the blasphemy: "the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles through you" — Gill notes the being, perfections, law, and worship of God are denied or evil spoken of; the name of God blasphemed; the holy name reproached; the dishonour to God. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the cause: the Jews' conduct led the heathen to blaspheme both their religion and its Author — by their hypocrisy and crimes the pagans despised a religion observed to have no effect in purifying its professors, and the reproach terminated on its Author; the inconsistent life causing the blasphemy; the hypocrisy the occasion. The witnesses press the dreadful effect of the professing people's sin: the sin of God's professing people brought reproach on God himself — the Gentiles judging the religion by its professors, and despising the God whose people lived so; the dreadful effect; the dishonour to God through his people; the reproach on the holy name. The expositors draw the lesson on the inconsistent life: the inconsistent life of the professing people dishonours God before the world; the hypocrisy that reproaches the holy name; the believer's sin a stumbling-block; "by reason of whom the way of truth shall be evil spoken of" (2 Peter 2:2); the dishonour through inconsistency. The witnesses observe the appeal to Scripture: "as it is written" — Paul cites the Scripture (Isaiah 52:5; Ezekiel 36:20-23), where God's name was blasphemed through his people's sin; the charge grounded in the prophets; the Scripture confirming; the written word. The expositors note the solemn warning: the verse warns the professing people that their inconsistency brings reproach on God; the warning to live worthily; the holy name to be honoured by the holy life; the responsibility of the professor; the warning against hypocrisy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the name of God blasphemed, the reproach through hypocrisy, and the inconsistent life dishonouring God. The verse thus records God's name blasphemed among the Gentiles through the Jews' sin — teaching the dreadful effect of the professing people's sin, bringing reproach on God himself.
25:
"For circumcision verily profiteth, if thou keep the law: but if thou be a breaker of the law, thy circumcision is made uncircumcision." The witnesses note that circumcision profits only with obedience. Gill notes the apostle allows that circumcision was profitable — understood while it was in force, before its abrogation; but it was only profitable in case the law was kept. Barnes notes circumcision was the rite by which the relation to the covenant of Abraham was recognised. The witnesses note Paul granting that circumcision profits if the law is kept, but declaring that to the law-breaker it becomes as uncircumcision — the outward sign worthless without the obedience it signified; the rite that does not avail the transgressor; the privilege voided by disobedience. The witnesses note circumcision profiting with obedience. The witnesses note the sign voided by sin.

The witnesses note circumcision profiting only with obedience — to the law-breaker the sign becomes as uncircumcision.

So the witnesses make the verse circumcision profiting only with obedience.
