The lesson the expositors draw is that the outward sign is worthless without the obedience it signified, the privilege voided by disobedience. The witnesses note the profit conditioned: "circumcision verily profiteth, if thou keep the law" — Gill notes the apostle allows circumcision was profitable (while in force, before its abrogation), but only if the law was kept; the profit conditioned on obedience; the sign valuable with the reality. The expositors observe the voiding by transgression: "if thou be a breaker of the law, thy circumcision is made uncircumcision" — to the law-breaker, the circumcision becomes as uncircumcision; the sign voided; the rite worthless to the transgressor; the privilege made nothing by disobedience. The witnesses press the worthlessness of the outward sign without the reality: circumcision (the outward sign of the covenant) is worthless without the obedience it signified; the sign without the substance; the outward without the inward; the rite empty without the reality; the form without the power (v.20). The expositors draw the lesson on the sign and the thing signified: the outward sign profits only with the inward reality; circumcision pointed to the circumcision of the heart (v.29); the sign without the thing signified is void; the rite without the reality worthless; the sacrament without the grace. The witnesses observe the privilege voided by sin: the Jew's circumcision, his covenant privilege, is voided by his law-breaking; the privilege made nothing by disobedience; the advantage forfeited by sin; the sign turned to uncircumcision; the privilege without obedience void. The expositors note the principle applied to all outward privilege: the principle reaches all outward signs and privileges — they profit only with the inward reality; the baptism, the profession, the rite, void without the grace; the outward without the inward worthless; the principle universal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks circumcision profiting with obedience, the sign voided by sin, and the outward worthless without the inward. The verse thus records circumcision profiting only with obedience — to the law-breaker as uncircumcision — teaching that the outward sign is worthless without the obedience it signified.
26:
"Therefore if the uncircumcision keep the righteousness of the law, shall not his uncircumcision be counted for circumcision?" The witnesses note the converse — the uncircumcised who keeps the law. Gill notes the words are spoken hypothetically — if any such could be found among the Gentiles who keep the law; and can only be absolutely understood of those who from a principle of grace act in obedience, as in the hands of Christ, looking to him as the fulfilling end of the law for righteousness. Barnes notes the heathen who keeps the righteousness of the law. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if the uncircumcised keeps the righteousness of the law, his uncircumcision shall be counted for circumcision — the reality without the sign accepted where the sign without the reality is rejected; the obedient Gentile reckoned as the true circumcised. The witnesses note uncircumcision counted for circumcision. The witnesses note the reality over the sign.

The witnesses note the uncircumcised who keeps the law counted for circumcision — the reality without the sign accepted over the sign without the reality.

So the witnesses make the verse the converse of the obedient uncircumcised. The lesson the expositors draw is that the reality without the sign is accepted where the sign without the reality is rejected. The witnesses note the converse: "if the uncircumcision keep the righteousness of the law, shall not his uncircumcision be counted for circumcision?" — Paul declares that if the uncircumcised keeps the law's righteousness, his uncircumcision shall be counted for circumcision; the converse of v.25; the reality over the sign. The expositors observe Gill's note on the hypothetical and its true sense: the words are spoken hypothetically (if any such Gentile could be found who keeps the law), and can only be absolutely understood of those who from a principle of grace act in obedience, in the hands of Christ, looking to him as the fulfilling end of the law for righteousness; the hypothetical pointing to the believing Gentile; the obedience of grace. The witnesses press the reality over the sign: the uncircumcised who keeps the law's righteousness is counted as circumcised — the reality (the obedient heart) accepted without the sign, where the sign (circumcision) without the reality is rejected (v.25); the reality over the sign; the substance over the form; the heart over the rite. The expositors draw the lesson on the acceptance of the reality: God accepts the reality (the obedient, believing heart) even without the outward sign, and rejects the sign without the reality; the substance, not the form; the heart, not the rite; "circumcision is that of the heart" (v.29); the reality accepted. The witnesses observe the application to the Gentile believer: the believing Gentile, who by grace keeps the righteousness of the law (in Christ, and by the Spirit), is counted as the true circumcised; the Gentile believer accepted; the uncircumcised reckoned as circumcised; the door open to the Gentile. The expositors note the levelling of the privilege: the verse levels the Jewish privilege — the obedient Gentile accepted, the disobedient Jew rejected; the privilege not the ground; the reality the measure; the no-respect-of-persons (v.11) applied to the sign. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the uncircumcision counted for circumcision, the reality over the sign, and the obedient Gentile accepted. The verse thus records the uncircumcised who keeps the law counted for circumcision — teaching that the reality without the sign is accepted where the sign without the reality is rejected.
27:
"And shall not uncircumcision which is by nature, if it fulfil the law, judge thee, who by the letter and circumcision dost transgress the law?" The witnesses note the obedient Gentile judging the disobedient Jew. Gill notes the Gentiles are by nature uncircumcised, as circumcision was by command and performed by art, but uncircumcision is by nature, what men naturally have. Barnes notes that if the uncircumcised keep the law, their conduct condemns the Jew — as a more consistent and upright man condemns us by his example. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the naturally uncircumcised who fulfills the law shall judge the Jew who, with the letter and circumcision, transgresses it — the obedient Gentile a standing rebuke to the privileged law-breaker; the reality condemning the empty sign. The witnesses note the Gentile judging the Jew. The witnesses note the reality rebuking the form.

The witnesses note the obedient Gentile judging the disobedient Jew — the reality a standing rebuke to the empty privilege.

So the witnesses make the verse the obedient Gentile judging the disobedient Jew. The lesson the expositors draw is that the obedient Gentile is a standing rebuke to the privileged law-breaker, the reality condemning the empty sign. The witnesses note the uncircumcision by nature: "uncircumcision which is by nature" — Gill notes the Gentiles are by nature uncircumcised (circumcision being by command and art, uncircumcision by nature); the natural state; the Gentile without the rite; the uncircumcised by birth. The expositors observe the judging: "if it fulfil the law, judge thee, who by the letter and circumcision dost transgress the law" — Barnes notes that if the uncircumcised keep the law, their conduct condemns the Jew — as a more upright man condemns us by his example; the obedient Gentile judging the disobedient Jew; the reality condemning the form. The witnesses press the rebuke of the reality to the form: the Gentile who fulfils the law (in reality, though without the sign) judges the Jew who has the letter and circumcision (the form) but transgresses; the reality rebuking the empty form; the obedience condemning the bare privilege; the substance judging the shell. The expositors draw the lesson on the condemnation by example: the obedient, though uncircumcised, condemns the disobedient, though circumcised — as the consistent life rebukes the inconsistent; the condemnation by example; the upright shaming the hypocrite; "the men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment" (Matthew 12:41); the reality judging the form. The witnesses observe "the letter and circumcision": the Jew had "the letter" (the written law) and circumcision (the sign), yet transgressed; the outward possession without the inward reality; the letter without the spirit (v.29); the form without the substance; the privilege without the obedience. The expositors note the overturning of the privilege: the verse overturns the Jewish confidence — the privileged law-breaker judged by the unprivileged law-keeper; the privilege no defense; the reality the measure; the boast overturned; the form condemned by the substance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Gentile judging the Jew, the reality rebuking the form, and the condemnation by example. The verse thus records the obedient Gentile judging the disobedient Jew — teaching that the obedient Gentile is a standing rebuke to the privileged law-breaker, the reality condemning the empty sign.
28:
"For he is not a Jew, which is one outwardly; neither is that circumcision, which is outward in the flesh:" The witnesses note that the true Jew is not the merely outward one. Gill notes the apostle removes the plea from name and privilege by distinguishing between a Jew and a Jew, and between circumcision and circumcision: he is not a Jew who is one outwardly — by mere name, nature, nation, religion, and profession. Barnes notes that he who is merely descended from Abraham, circumcised, and externally conforming to the law, does not possess the true character contemplated by the separation of the Jewish people. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the merely outward Jew is not the true Jew, nor is the merely outward circumcision the true circumcision — the rejection of the outward, fleshly profession as the ground of acceptance; the true Jew defined by reality, not by name and rite. The witnesses note not the outward Jew. The witnesses note not the outward circumcision.

The witnesses note the true Jew is not the merely outward one — rejecting the fleshly profession as the ground of acceptance.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejection of the merely outward Jew. The lesson the expositors draw is that the true Jew is defined by reality, not by name and rite, the outward profession not the ground of acceptance. The witnesses note the negative: "he is not a Jew, which is one outwardly" — Gill notes the apostle distinguishes between a Jew and a Jew: he is not a Jew who is one outwardly — by mere name, nature, nation, religion, and profession; the merely outward Jew not the true Jew; the name without the reality. The expositors observe the circumcision: "neither is that circumcision, which is outward in the flesh" — the merely outward circumcision (the fleshly rite) is not the true circumcision; the outward sign not the reality; the rite without the thing signified; the fleshly mark not the true circumcision. The witnesses press the rejection of the outward as the ground: the merely outward — the name, the descent, the rite, the profession — is not the ground of acceptance; the outward rejected as the ground; the profession not the reality; the form not the substance; "they are not all Israel, which are of Israel" (Romans 9:6). The expositors draw the lesson on the true Jew defined by reality: the true Jew is not the merely outward one, but the one with the inward reality (v.29); the true Israel defined by grace, not descent; the reality, not the name; the heart, not the flesh; the substance the mark. The witnesses observe Barnes's note on the true character: he who is merely descended from Abraham, circumcised, and externally conforming does not possess the true character contemplated by the separation of the Jewish people — that required much more; the outward not the design; the true character the inward; the separation pointing beyond the flesh. The expositors note the principle for all profession: the principle reaches all outward religion — the merely outward professor is not the true believer; the name, the rite, the profession not the ground; the reality required; the outward insufficient; the principle universal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks not the outward Jew, not the outward circumcision, and the reality over the profession. The verse thus records that the true Jew is not the merely outward one — teaching that the true Jew is defined by reality, not by name and rite.
29:
"But he is a Jew, which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit, and not in the letter; whose praise is not of men, but of God." The witnesses note the true Jew and the circumcision of the heart. Gill notes the true Jew has an internal work of grace upon his soul — not only an outward name but an inward nature, not the law in the hand but in the heart, not an external righteousness only but internal holiness, a worshipper of God in Spirit and in truth. Barnes notes such a one comes up to the design of the Jewish institution, having the true spirit. The witnesses note Paul defining the true Jew as one inwardly, whose circumcision is of the heart, in the spirit not the letter, whose praise is of God not men — the inward, spiritual reality that the outward sign foreshadowed; the true people of God marked by the renewed heart, not the fleshly rite. The witnesses note the Jew inwardly. The witnesses note circumcision of the heart.

The witnesses note the true Jew inwardly and the circumcision of the heart — the inward, spiritual reality the sign foreshadowed.

So the witnesses make the verse the true Jew and the circumcision of the heart. The lesson the expositors draw is the inward, spiritual reality that the outward sign foreshadowed, the true people of God marked by the renewed heart. The witnesses note the true Jew: "he is a Jew, which is one inwardly" — Gill notes the true Jew has an internal work of grace upon his soul — not only an outward name but an inward nature, a worshipper of God in Spirit and in truth; Barnes notes such a one comes up to the design of the Jewish institution; the inward Jew; the reality; the true Israelite. The expositors observe the circumcision of the heart: "circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit, and not in the letter" — the true circumcision is of the heart, wrought by the Spirit, not the outward letter; the inward circumcision; the heart cut off from sin; the spiritual reality; "circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart" (Deuteronomy 10:16; 30:6). The witnesses press the spirit not the letter: "in the spirit, and not in the letter" — the true circumcision is in the spirit (the Spirit's work), not the letter (the outward rite); the spirit over the letter; the reality over the form. The expositors draw the lesson on the inward reality the sign foreshadowed: the outward circumcision foreshadowed the inward — the cutting off of sin, the renewal of the heart; the sign pointing to the thing signified; the rite to the reality; the flesh to the spirit; the true circumcision the heart's renewal. The witnesses observe the praise of God not men: "whose praise is not of men, but of God" — the true Jew's praise is from God (who sees the heart), not from men (who see the outward); there is a play on the name "Judah/Jew" (meaning praise); the praise of God, not men; the approval that matters; the divine commendation. The expositors note the true people of God defined: the true people of God are marked by the renewed heart, the inward reality, the Spirit's work — not the fleshly rite, the outward profession; the true Israel; the circumcision of the heart; the people of God by grace; the new covenant reality. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jew inwardly, the circumcision of the heart, and the praise of God not men. The verse thus records the true Jew and the circumcision of the heart — teaching the inward, spiritual reality that the outward sign foreshadowed, the true people of God marked by the renewed heart, whose praise is of God.
